\begin{document}
\title{Agreement coefficients with missing raters}
\author{Jonas Moss}
\date{\today}
\address{Department of Data Science and Analytics, BI Norwegian Business School,
Oslo, Norway}
\begin{abstract}
Missing data is a pervasive challenge in studies of inter-rater agreement,
yet common agreement coefficients, such as Conger’s kappa, Fleiss’
kappa, and the Brennan--Prediger coefficient, are often applied without
clear justification when ratings are incomplete. We develop a general
framework for handling missing data in agreement analysis that supports
both categorical and continuous responses, allows arbitrary disagreement
functions, and hold provided the data is pairwise missing completely
at random. Then we characterize when pairwise-available estimators
remain consistent, derive their correct standard errors, and identify
settings where conventional formulas fail. We then propose reweighted
kappa estimators that remain valid under substantially weaker assumptions.
Asymptotic covariance matrices enable joint inference across coefficients,
and simulations verify the theoretical claims. The methods are implemented
in an R package $\mathtt{misskappa}$ designed for applied use.
\end{abstract}

\maketitle

\section{Introduction}

A chance-corrected agreement coefficient is any coefficient on the
form $\kappa_{x}=1-\frac{d}{d_{x}}$ where $d$ is a measure of disagreement
and $d_{x}$ a measure of chance disagreement. Let $X^{\star}$ be
a random vector with $R$ components. The vector contains all rating
data, including potentially unobserved ratings. Define disagreement
as
\begin{equation}
d=\binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})],\label{eq:population disagreement}
\end{equation}
where $l(x,y)\geq0$ is a symmetric loss function satisfying $l(x,x)=0$.
The by far most common are the nominal ($l(x,y)=1[x\neq y]$), absolute
value ($l(x,y)=|x-y|$), and quadratic ($l(x,y)=(x-y)^{2}$) losses.

We cover three variants of chance disagreement, the Fleiss-type \citep{Fleiss1971-to},
Cohen-type \citep{Cohen1968-sb}, and Brennan--Prediger-type \citep{Brennan1981-kf}.
These are defined as
\begin{eqnarray}
d_{F} & = & R^{-2}\sum_{j,k}E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})],\label{eq:fleiss disagreement}\\
d_{C} & = & \binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})],\label{eq:cohendisagreement}\\
d_{BP} & = & C^{-2}\sum_{a,b}l(a,b),\label{eq:bp disagreement}
\end{eqnarray}
where $X_{i\cdot}^{\star}$ and $X_{i'\cdot}^{\star}$ are independent
samples from the same distribution. In the case of Brennan--Prediger
we must assume that $X^{\star}$ takes on $C<\infty$ categorical
values. Interpretations of these chance disagreements are well-known
and can be found elsewhere.

For complete data, the kappa coefficients are estimated using the
natural sample counterparts. Their standard errors may be found using
either multinomial theory or $U$-statistics \citep{Moss2024-cc}. 

Our task is to estimate and do inference on agreement coefficients
in the presence of missing or incomplete data. When dealing with incomplete
data we do not observe all ratings in$X^{\star}$. We formalize this
by defining
\[
X_{j}=\begin{cases}
X_{j}^{\star} & M_{j}=1,\\
\text{NA} & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
 where $M$ is the missingness indicator and $\text{NA}$ denotes
a missing value. We naturally assume that $X_{j}^{\star}$ cannot
take on the value $\text{NA}$. 

We w

\section{Hájek-type inverse probability-weighted estimator}

We begin by describing an inverse probability-weighted estimator of
the Hájek-type. 

\subsection{Definition}

Define the observation probabilities $\pi_{j}=P(M_{j}=1)$ and $\pi_{jk}=P(M_{j}M_{k}=1)$.
Then define the natural sample estimators $\hat{\pi}_{j}=\hat{P}(M_{j}=1)$
and $\hat{\pi}_{jk}=\hat{P}(M_{j}M_{k}=1)$, with $\hat{P}$ being
the empirical probabilities. 

The \emph{Hájek-type inverse probability-weighted estimator} is $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{IPW}=1-\hat{d}^{IPW}/\hat{d}_{C}^{IPW}$,
where we define
\begin{eqnarray*}
\hat{d}^{IPW} & = & \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij},X_{ik})M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1}},\\
\hat{d}_{C}^{IPW} & = & \frac{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij},X_{i'k})M_{ij}M_{i'k}\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}}{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{i'k}\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}},
\end{eqnarray*}
with $\hat{d}_{F}^{IPW}$being defined analogously to $\hat{d}_{C}^{IPW}$,
with summation over all $j,k$. This is the natural extension of the
Hájek estimator to the agreement setting. 

It follows from the strong law of large numbers, Slutsky's theorem,
and the continuous mapping theorem \citep[see, e.g.,][]{Van_der_Vaart2000-qc}
that
\begin{eqnarray}
\hat{d}^{IPW} & \stackrel{a.s.}{\to} & \binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}\pi_{jk}^{-1}E[l(X_{1j},X_{1k})M_{1j}M_{1k}],\label{eq:d_IPW}\\
\hat{d}_{C}^{IPW} & \stackrel{a.s.}{\to} & \binom{R}{2}^{-1}\sum_{j<k}\pi_{j}^{-1}\pi_{k}^{-1}E[l(X_{1j},X_{2k})M_{1j}M_{2k}],\nonumber 
\end{eqnarray}
with a similar limit for $\hat{d}_{F}^{IPW}$. An obvious sufficient
condition for $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{IPW}$ to be consistent is that $\pi_{jk}^{-1}E[l(X_{ij},X_{ik})M_{ij}M_{ik}]$
equals $E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})]$ and that $\pi_{j}^{-1}\pi_{k}^{-1}E[l(X_{ij},X_{i'k})M_{ij}M_{i'k}]$
equals $E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})]$, and these conditions
hold when the data is missing completely at random. We also require
that $d_{X}>0$ for $\kappa_{X}$ to be defined, and that all $\pi_{jk}>0$,
so all rater combinations are counted.
\begin{prop}
\label{prop:hajek estimator}The estimators $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{IPW}$
are strongly consistent for all $X\in\{C,F,BP\}$ when the data is
missing completely at random, exchangeable raters or not, provided
that $d_{X}>0$ and all $\pi_{jk}>0$. 
\end{prop}
\begin{proof}
That $\pi_{jk}^{-1}E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{ik}]=E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})]$
is equivalent to $E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})]=E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})|M_{ij}M_{ik}=1]$,
which holds true when data is missing completely at random. The same
reasoning holds for $\pi_{j}^{-1}\pi_{k}^{-1}E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{i'k}]=E[l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})]$
as well. 
\end{proof}

\paragraph*{Motivation sketch}

Proposition \ref{prop:hajek estimator} tells us that $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{IPW}$
is strongly consistent. This is nice, but we usually desire our estimators
to be efficient. It is well-known form semi-parametric efficiency
theory that this estimator is not efficient \citep{Tsiatis2006-sx}.
Constructing efficient estimators either require complex semi-parametric
machinery for general data or iterative methods such as the EM-algorithm
for categorical data. We intend to explore the EM-algorithm in a future
paper, but will remark that semiparametric methods for efficiency
gains is likely to backfire, and the complexity is not worth the investment
in our situation.

Our motivation for the Hájek-type estimator cannot be based on efficiency
directly, but we can make a weaker case for it. If we construct the
population values $\kappa_{X}^{IPW}$ using the limits in equation
\ref{eq:d_IPW}, the plugin-estimator $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{IPW}$ is
trivially efficient from standard theory. Moreover, it is consistent
under reasonable assumptions that are weaker than missing completely
at random. This is more than what we can say of its competitors

\subsection{On inference}

The estimators $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{IPW}$ are asymptotically normal
under the mild condition that the random values $l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1}$
have finite second moments. For each $X\in\{C,F,BP\}$, we have that
\[
\sqrt{n}\big(\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{IPW}-\kappa_{X}^{IPW}\big)\stackrel{d}{\to}N(0,\sigma_{X}^{2}),
\]
where $\kappa_{X}^{IPW}$ is the almost sure limit of $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{IPW}$,
which equals $\kappa_{X}$ under the conditions of Proposition \ref{prop:hajek estimator}.
The asymptotic variance $\sigma_{X}^{2}$, can be expressed using
an (efficient) influence function in the observed-data model, and
we estimate it by plugging in estimated influence functions.
\begin{equation}
\phi_{\kappa_{X}}=-d_{X}^{-1}\phi_{d}+d\,d_{X}^{-2}\phi_{d_{X}}.\label{eq:influence}
\end{equation}
To make this operational, write the IPW estimators as ratios. For
the observed disagreement,$\hat{d}^{IPW}=\hat{\psi}_{dN}/\hat{\psi}_{dD}$
where
\begin{eqnarray*}
\hat{\psi}_{dN} & = & \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}h_{dN,i},\quad h_{dN,i}=\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij},X_{ik})M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1},\\
\hat{\psi}_{dD} & = & \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}h_{dD,i},\quad h_{dD,i}=\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{ik}\hat{\pi}_{jk}^{-1}.
\end{eqnarray*}
Then
\begin{eqnarray*}
\hat{\phi}_{d,i} & = & \hat{\psi}_{dD}^{-1}\big(\hat{\phi}_{dN,i}-\hat{d}^{IPW}\hat{\phi}_{dD,i}\big),\\
\hat{\phi}_{dN,i} & = & h_{dN,i}-\hat{\psi}_{dN},\\
\hat{\phi}_{dD,i} & = & h_{dD,i}-\hat{\psi}_{dD}.
\end{eqnarray*}
Similarly, for $X\in\{C,F\}$ write$\hat{d}_{X}^{IPW}=\hat{\psi}_{XN}/\hat{\psi}_{XD}$
with $\hat{\psi}_{XN},\hat{\psi}_{XD}$ defined by the corresponding
double sums in the definition of $\hat{d}_{X}^{IPW}$. Let $K_{XN}$
and $K_{XD}$ denote the associated kernels, and define $Z_{i}=(X_{i\cdot},M_{i\cdot})$
so that
\[
\hat{\psi}_{X\bullet}=\frac{1}{n^{2}}\sum_{i,i'}K_{X\bullet}\big((X_{i\cdot},M_{i\cdot}),(X_{i'\cdot},M_{i'\cdot})\big),\qquad X\bullet\in\{XN,XD\}.
\]
Estimate their influence functions by the usual $V$-statistic projections:
\begin{eqnarray*}
\hat{\phi}_{X\bullet,i} & = & \big(\hat{g}_{X\bullet,1,i}-\hat{\psi}_{X\bullet}\big)+\big(\hat{g}_{X\bullet,2,i}-\hat{\psi}_{X\bullet}\big),\\
\hat{g}_{X\bullet,1,i} & = & \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i'}K_{X\bullet}(Z_{i},Z_{i'}),\\
\hat{g}_{X\bullet,2,i} & = & \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i'}K_{X\bullet}(Z_{i'},Z_{i}),
\end{eqnarray*}
and then set $\hat{\phi}_{d_{X},i}=\hat{\psi}_{XD}^{-1}\big(\hat{\phi}_{XN,i}-\hat{d}_{X}^{IPW}\hat{\phi}_{XD,i}\big).$
For the Brennan--Prediger coefficient, $d_{X}=d_{BP}$ is constant,
so $\phi_{d_{X}}=0$. 

A consistent estimator of $\sigma_{X}^{2}$ is $\hat{\sigma}_{X}^{2}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\hat{\phi}_{\kappa_{X},i}^{2},$and
the same construction yields a joint asymptotic covariance matrix
by replacing the square with an outer product. The same influence-function
formalism applies to the available-case and Gwet-estimators, introduced
below, by substituting their corresponding weights in the definitions
of $\hat{d}$ and $\hat{d}_{X}$. We may use the same influence functions
for the covariance between agreement coefficients as well.

\subsection{Computation }

\subsection{Illustrations}

\section{Other estimators}

For context and comparison, we record two simple estimators that are
commonly encountered in applied work and software for incomplete ratings.
They are useful baselines and reference points when discussing robustness
and inconsistency under missing at random (MAR).

\subsection{Gwet's estimator (irrCAC)}

The default missing-data behavior in the popular $\mathtt{R}$ package
$\mathtt{irrCAC}$ corresponds to a Gwet-style reweighting: it keeps
the observed disagreement component but changes the chance disagreement
component to compensate for unequal rater observation probabilities.
In our notation we define, for each $X\in\{C,F,BP\}$, $\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{G}=1-\hat{d}^{G}/\hat{d}_{X}^{G}$,
where the observed disagreement is the same as the available-case
estimator,
\[
\hat{d}^{G}=\hat{d}^{AC}=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{ik}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{ik}}.
\]
For the chance disagreement we use empirical rater observation probabilities
$\hat{\pi}_{j}=\hat{P}(M_{j}=1)$ and define
\begin{eqnarray*}
\hat{d}_{C}^{G} & = & \frac{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{i'k}\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}}{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{i'k}\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}},
\end{eqnarray*}
with $\hat{d}_{F}^{G}$ being the obvious modification and $d_{BP}$
as in equation \ref{eq:bp disagreement}.

\subsection{Available-case estimator}

The available-case (pairwise complete) estimator is a natural baseline
when ratings are incomplete. It uses only the observed rater pairs
and treats all observed pairs equally. We may define the available
case Conger's kappa estimator as $\hat{\kappa}_{C}^{AC}=1-\hat{d}^{AC}/\hat{d}_{C}^{AC}$,
where 
\begin{eqnarray*}
\hat{d}^{AC} & = & \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{ik}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{ik}},\quad\hat{d}_{C}^{AC}=\frac{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{i'k}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{i'k}}{\sum_{i,i'}\sum_{j<k}M_{ij}M_{i'k}}.
\end{eqnarray*}
Observe that $l(X_{ij}^{\star},X_{ik}^{\star})M_{ij}M_{ik}=l(X_{ij},X_{ik})$
if we extend the loss $l$ to satisfy $l(x,y)=0$ when $x$ or $y$
is $\text{NA}$. 

\subsection{Properties of available-case and Gwet}

We summarize basic consistency properties of the available-case and
Gwet estimators under simple missingness assumptions.
\begin{defn}
\label{def:missingness} Let $X^{\star}$ be a random vector of size
$R$ and $X$ be the observed incomplete data. The components of $X$
are missing \emph{uniformly at random} if the component-wise missingness
probabilities are constant (i.e., $P(M_{i})=\pi$ for all $i$) and
$M_{i}$ and $M_{j}$ are independent.
\end{defn}
\begin{prop}
\label{prop:The-available-case}The available case estimator is strongly
consistent if either
\begin{enumerate}
\item The raters are exchangeable and data is missing completely at random.
\item The raters are not exchangeable and data is missing uniformly at random.
\end{enumerate}
\end{prop}
Under the same two conditions,$\hat{\kappa}_{X}^{G}$ is strongly
consistent as well.
\begin{prop}
Both estimators are in general not consistent when data is missing
at random, even when raters are exchangeable. 
\end{prop}

\subsection{Data for Fleiss' kappa on count form}

This section is included for completeness. We do not propose any new
methodology for Fleiss-form count data, but only clarify what can
(and cannot) be inferred when rater identities are unavailable.

Until now we have discussed estimators that require data on the form
$X^{*}\in\mathbb{R}^{J}$ (raw ratings, with one column per rater).
In many applications the data is only available on Fleiss' count form:
for each subject $i$ we observe category counts $(N_{i1},\ldots,N_{iC})$
with $\sum_{a=1}^{C}N_{ia}=R_{i}$ and no rater identities. This aggregation
makes Fleiss' kappa well-defined under exchangeability, but it also
removes the information needed to define rater-specific observation
probabilities $\pi_{j}$ and hence rater-specific estimators defined
for raw data, such as the available-case estimator.

An illustration is provided by the data from Fleiss' original paper
\citep{Fleiss1971-to}, available as $\mathtt{dat.fleiss1971}$ in
the $\mathtt{misskappa}$ package. It contains $n=30$ subjects, $C=5$
categories, and $R_{i}=6$ diagnoses per subject. Importantly, the
set of six psychiatrists is not the same for all patients, which is
naturally represented by counts but would correspond to a sparse raw
rating matrix if all psychiatrists were indexed.

For these data the non-parametric estimator yields $\hat{\kappa}_{F}=0.430$,
with Wald standard error 0.053 and a 95 percent confidence interval
{[}0.326, 0.535{]}.

\section{Simulations}

We use a small Monte Carlo study to illustrate (i) settings where
the available-case estimator behaves well, (ii) settings where it
can be badly biased even under MCAR, and (iii) settings where all
simple reweighting approaches fail under MAR.The simulation design
is described in the table note.

In particular, under non-exchangeable raters with unequal observation
probabilities (DGP B), the available-case and Gwet-style estimators
are biased, while the Hájek-type IPW estimator is approximately unbiased.When
missingness depends on latent item difficulty (DGP C), all three estimators
target a reweighted parameter and exhibit similar bias.

\input{tables/simulations-raw-three-estimators-main.tex}

Additional simulation tables (linear/absolute loss, and Fleiss/Brennan-{}-Prediger
variants) are in the online supplement, \texttt{supplement/simulations-raw-three-estimators-appendix.tex}.

\section{The $\mathtt{R}$ package $\mathtt{misskappa}$}

All methods proposed in this paper are implemented in the $\mathtt{misskappa}$
package. The main entry points are $\mathtt{kappa\_raw}$ for raw
categorical rating matrices (subjects $\times$ raters, with missing
values encoded as $\text{NA}$), and $\mathtt{kappa\_counts}$ for
Fleiss-form count data.

Example (raw ratings with missing values). The dataset $\mathtt{dat.gwet2014}$
included with the package contains $n=20$ items rated by $R=5$ raters
with several missing ratings. The following code computes agreement
under the three incomplete-data estimators discussed in the paper.\begin{verbatim}library(misskappa)data(dat.gwet2014)x <- as.matrix(dat.gwet2014)kappa_raw(x, method = "available", weight = "identity")  # ACkappa_raw(x, method = "ipw",       weight = "identity")  # Hajek-type IPWkappa_raw(x, method = "gwet",      weight = "identity")  # Gwet (comparison)\end{verbatim}

Example (Fleiss-form counts). The dataset $\mathtt{dat.fleiss1971}$
provides Fleiss' original count-form example (Table 1 in \citep{Fleiss1971-to}).
Fleiss' kappa (and Brennan-{}-Prediger) can be computed directly from
counts via\begin{verbatim}library(misskappa)data(dat.fleiss1971)# r is the number of diagnoses per subject (here, r = 6).kappa_counts(dat.fleiss1971, method = "available", weight = "identity", r = 6)\end{verbatim}

\section{Concluding remarks}

\appendix

\section{Appendix: Proofs}

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:The-available-case}}
\begin{proof}[of Proposition \ref{prop:The-available-case}]
We sketch the argument for $\hat{\kappa}_{C}^{AC}=1-\hat{d}^{AC}/\hat{d}_{C}^{AC}$;
the same reasoning applies to the corresponding Fleiss and Brennan--Prediger
versions. Define
\[
C_{jk}(x^{\star})=P(M_{j}M_{k}=1\mid X^{\star}=x^{\star}),\qquad C_{j}(x^{\star})=P(M_{j}=1\mid X^{\star}=x^{\star}).
\]
Then
\[
E\!\big[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})M_{j}M_{k}\big]=E\!\big[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})C_{jk}(X^{\star})\big],\qquad E[M_{j}M_{k}]=E[C_{jk}(X^{\star})].
\]
By the law of large numbers and the continuous mapping theorem,
\[
\hat{d}^{AC}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}\frac{\sum_{j<k}E\!\big[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})C_{jk}(X^{\star})\big]}{\sum_{j<k}E[C_{jk}(X^{\star})]}.
\]
Next, let $Y^{\star}$ be an independent copy of $X^{\star}$. A similar
calculation yields
\[
\hat{d}_{C}^{AC}\stackrel{a.s.}{\to}\frac{\sum_{j<k}E\!\big[l(X_{j}^{\star},Y_{k}^{\star})C_{j}(X^{\star})C_{k}(Y^{\star})\big]}{\sum_{j<k}E[C_{j}(X^{\star})]E[C_{k}(Y^{\star})]},
\]
where the $i=i'$ terms are negligible relative to the $i\neq i'$
terms. Therefore $\hat{\kappa}_{C}^{AC}$ converges to the corresponding
ratio-of-limits.If the raters are exchangeable and the data are missing
completely at random, then $C_{jk}(X^{\star})=\pi_{jk}$ and $C_{j}(X^{\star})=\pi_{j}$
are constants, while exchangeability implies $E[l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})]$
and $E[l(X_{j}^{\star},Y_{k}^{\star})]$ do not depend on the indices.
Hence the constants $\pi_{jk}$ and $\pi_{j}\pi_{k}$ cancel in the
limits and we obtain $\hat{d}^{AC}\to d$ and $\hat{d}_{C}^{AC}\to d_{C}$,
so $\hat{\kappa}_{C}^{AC}\to\kappa_{C}$. If raters are not exchangeable
but the missingness is uniformly at random (Definition \ref{def:missingness}),
then the observation probabilities are constant across indices, so
the limits again reduce to the unweighted averages defining $d$ and
$d_{C}$.Finally, the displayed limits show why inconsistency is typical
under more general mechanisms: if $C_{jk}(X^{\star})$ or $C_{j}(X^{\star})$
depends on $X^{\star}$ in a way that is correlated with $l(X_{j}^{\star},X_{k}^{\star})$
or $l(X_{j}^{\star},Y_{k}^{\star})$, then the probability limits
target reweighted versions of $d$ and $d_{C}$rather than the desired
parameters.
\end{proof}
\begin{rem}
The same limit calculation can be carried out for Gwet's estimator
as defined in the main text by replacing the chance-disagreement sums
with their $\hat{\pi}_{j}^{-1}\hat{\pi}_{k}^{-1}$ reweighted versions.
Under MCAR the reweighting cancels (since $C_{j}(X^{\star})=\pi_{j}$),
but under more general mechanisms where $C_{j}(X^{\star})$ depends
on $X^{\star}$, the probability limit of the chance term is generally
a reweighted target and need not equal $d_{C}$ or $d_{F}$.
\end{rem}

\end{document}
